note: A country dance song of obscure meaning — 'popping the weasel' may reference pawning a tailor's iron. Public domain across all its traditional verses.
verses:
Half a pound of tuppenny rice,
Half a pound of treacle,
That's the way the money goes,
Pop! goes the weasel.

Up and down the City Road,
In and out the Eagle,
That's the way the money goes,
Pop! goes the weasel.

A penny for a spool of thread,
A penny for a needle,
That's the way the money goes,
Pop! goes the weasel.

title: The Girl I Left Behind Me
origin: Traditional Irish/British march tune, in use by British and American armies since at least the late 18th century; earliest printed lyrics c. 1791
note: A marching tune played by regiments departing for war on both sides of the Atlantic, from the American Revolution through the Civil War and beyond. Ancient and public domain.
verses:
I'm lonesome since I crossed the hill,
And o'er the moor and valley,
Such heavy thoughts my heart do fill,
Since parting with my Sally.
I seek no more the fine or gay,
For each does but remind me
How swift the hours did pass away
With the girl I left behind me.

Oh, ne'er shall I forget the night
The stars were bright above me,
And gently lent their silvery light
When first she vowed she loved me.
But now I'm bound to Brighton camp,
Good Lord, may weather find me,
And send me safely back again
To the girl I left behind me.

title: Turkey in the Straw
origin: Traditional American fiddle tune, melody traceable to the 1820s-30s Irish/British jig tradition; the innocuous "turkey" verses were widely circulated in print by the early 1900s
note: A staple of American fiddle music. The tune was also used for the separately-titled, racially offensive minstrel song "Zip Coon" (c. 1834) — the verses presented here are the unrelated, family-friendly "turkey" lyrics in standard circulation today, not that minstrel text.
verses:
Turkey in the straw, turkey in the hay,
Roll 'em up and twist 'em up a high tuckahaw,
And hit 'em up a tune called Turkey in the Straw.

Went out to milk, and I didn't know how,
I milked the goat instead of the cow,
A monkey sittin' on a pile of straw,
A-winkin' at his mother-in-law.

title: Skip to My Lou
origin: Traditional American play-party song, documented in the Midwest by the 1840s; first collected in print by the early 20th century
note: A 'play-party' song — sung a cappella for dancing games in communities where instrumental dance music was frowned upon. Public domain as an anonymous traditional song.
verses:
Lost my partner, what'll I do?
Lost my partner, what'll I do?
Lost my partner, what'll I do?
Skip to my Lou, my darling.

Skip, skip, skip to my Lou,
Skip, skip, skip to my Lou,
Skip, skip, skip to my Lou,
Skip to my Lou, my darling.

I'll get another one prettier than you,
I'll get another one prettier than you,
I'll get another one prettier than you,
Skip to my Lou, my darling.

title: She'll Be Comin' Round the Mountain
origin: Derived from the African American spiritual "When the Chariot Comes," published 1899 in William E. Barton's Old Plantation Hymns; secular verses adapted by Midwestern railroad workers in the 1890s, first printed in Carl Sandburg's The American Songbag, 1927
note: Traces to a spiritual about the Second Coming, reworked into a secular folk favorite. Both the underlying 1899 spiritual and the 1927 printed secular version are public domain.
verses:
She'll be comin' round the mountain when she comes,
She'll be comin' round the mountain when she comes,
She'll be comin' round the mountain,
She'll be comin' round the mountain,
She'll be comin' round the mountain when she comes.

She'll be drivin' six white horses when she comes,
She'll be drivin' six white horses when she comes,
She'll be drivin' six white horses,
She'll be drivin' six white horses,
She'll be drivin' six white horses when she comes.

We will all go out to meet her when she comes,
We will all go out to meet her when she comes,
We will all go out to meet her,
We will all go out to meet her,
We will all go out to meet her when she comes.

title: Buffalo Gals
origin: Written by John Hodges (performing as "Cool White") as "Lubly Fan," copyrighted 1844
note: Originally a minstrel-stage song later adapted into countless local variants ("Buffalo gals," "Alabama gals," etc.) as it traveled. The verses below are the commonly sung, non-dialect chorus and verse in standard circulation.
verses:
As I was walking down the street,
Down the street, down the street,
A pretty little girl I chanced to meet,
Under the silvery moon.

Buffalo gals, won't you come out tonight,
Come out tonight, come out tonight,
Buffalo gals, won't you come out tonight,
And dance by the light of the moon?

I asked her if she'd have a talk,
Have a talk, have a talk,
Her feet took up the whole sidewalk,
And left no room for me.

title: Old Dan Tucker
origin: Attributed to Dan Emmett, copyrighted 1843
note: One of the most popular songs of the 1840s minstrel stage, later absorbed into square dance and children's song repertoires. Presented here with the commonly sung, non-dialect verses in wide circulation today.
verses:
Old Dan Tucker was a fine old man,
Washed his face in a frying pan,
Combed his hair with a wagon wheel,
And died with a toothache in his heel.

Get out the way, old Dan Tucker,
You're too late to get your supper,
Supper's over and breakfast's cookin',
Old Dan Tucker just stands there lookin'.

Old Dan Tucker came to town,
Riding a billy goat, leading a hound,
The hound dog barked, the billy goat jumped,
And threw old Dan across a stump.

title: Jeanie with the Light Brown Hair
origin: Stephen Foster, published 1854 by Firth, Pond & Co., New York
note: Written for Foster's estranged wife Jane; became the official state song of Kentucky (as 'My Old Kentucky Home' companion) in the 20th century. Public domain since its 1854 publication.
verses:
I dream of Jeanie with the light brown hair,
Borne, like a vapor, on the summer air;
I see her tripping where the bright streams play,
Happy as the daisies that dance on her way.

Many were the wild notes her merry voice would pour,
Many were the blithe birds that warbled them o'er:
Oh! I dream of Jeanie with the light brown hair,
Floating, like a vapor, on the soft summer air.

I long for Jeanie with the daydawn smile,
Radiant in gladness, warm with winning guile;
I hear her melodies, like joys gone by,
Sighing round my heart o'er the fond hopes that die.

title: My Darling Nelly Gray
origin: Benjamin Russell Hanby, published 1856 by Oliver Ditson & Co., Boston
note: Written by an Ohio abolitionist minister's son about a slave family torn apart by sale, decades before the Civil War. Public domain since its 1856 publication.
verses:
There's a low green valley on the old Kentucky shore,
There I've whiled many happy hours away,
A-sitting and a-singing by the little cottage door,
Where lived my darling Nelly Gray.

Oh, my poor Nelly Gray, they have taken you away,
And I'll never see my darling any more;
I'm sitting by the river and I'm weeping all the day,
For you've gone from the old Kentucky shore.

One night I went to see her, but she's gone, the neighbors say,
The white man bound her with his chain;
They have taken her to Georgia for to wear her life away,
As she toils in the cotton and the cane.

title: When Johnny Comes Marching Home
origin: Patrick Sarsfield Gilmore, published 1863 (under the pen name Louis Lambert)
note: Written by a Union bandmaster, possibly adapted from the older Irish tune 'Johnny I Hardly Knew Ye.' Public domain since its 1863 publication.
verses:
When Johnny comes marching home again,
Hurrah! Hurrah!
We'll give him a hearty welcome then,
Hurrah! Hurrah!
The men will cheer, the boys will shout,
The ladies they will all turn out,
And we'll all feel gay
When Johnny comes marching home.

The old church bell will peal with joy,
Hurrah! Hurrah!
To welcome home our darling boy,
Hurrah! Hurrah!
The village lads and lassies say,
With roses they will strew the way,
And we'll all feel gay
When Johnny comes marching home.

Get ready for the jubilee,
Hurrah! Hurrah!
We'll give the hero three times three,
Hurrah! Hurrah!
The laurel wreath is ready now
To place upon his loyal brow,
And we'll all feel gay
When Johnny comes marching home.

title: Tenting on the Old Camp Ground
origin: Walter Kittredge, published 1864
note: Written by a Union soldier and popular on both sides of the Civil War as a weary plea for peace. Public domain since its 1860s publication.
verses:
We're tenting tonight on the old camp ground,
Give us a song to cheer
Our weary hearts, a song of home,
And friends we love so dear.

Many are the hearts that are weary tonight,
Wishing for the war to cease;
Many are the hearts looking for the right,
To see the dawn of peace.
Tenting tonight, tenting tonight,
Tenting on the old camp ground.

We've been tenting tonight on the old camp ground,
Thinking of days gone by,
Of the loved ones at home that gave us the hand,
And the tear that said goodbye.

title: The Battle Cry of Freedom
origin: George Frederick Root, published 1862 by Root & Cady, Chicago
note: One of the most popular songs of the Union Army, with a separate Confederate-lyric variant sung to the same tune. The 1862 original is public domain.
verses:
Yes, we'll rally round the flag, boys, we'll rally once again,
Shouting the battle cry of freedom,
We will rally from the hillside, we'll gather from the plain,
Shouting the battle cry of freedom.

The Union forever! Hurrah, boys, hurrah!
Down with the traitor, up with the star;
While we rally round the flag, boys, we rally once again,
Shouting the battle cry of freedom.

We are springing to the call of our brothers gone before,
Shouting the battle cry of freedom,
And we'll fill the vacant ranks with a million freemen more,
Shouting the battle cry of freedom.

title: Daisy Bell (A Bicycle Built for Two)
origin: Harry Dacre, published 1892
note: Written during the 1890s bicycle craze; later famous as the song IBM's computer HAL 9000 sings while being shut down in 2001: A Space Odyssey (1968). Public domain since 1892.
verses:
Daisy, Daisy, give me your answer, do!
I'm half crazy, all for the love of you!
It won't be a stylish marriage,
I can't afford a carriage,
But you'll look sweet upon the seat
Of a bicycle built for two.

We will go 'tandem' as man and wife,
Daisy, Daisy!
Peddling away down the road of life,
I and my Daisy Bell!
When the road's dark, we can both despise
P'licemen and lamps as well;
There are 'bright lights' in the dazzling eyes
Of beautiful Daisy Bell!

title: After the Ball
origin: Charles K. Harris, published 1891
note: One of the biggest hits of the 1890s, selling over five million copies of sheet music and helping establish Tin Pan Alley. Public domain since its 1891 publication.
verses:
A little maiden climbed an old man's knee,
Begged for a story, "Do, Uncle, please.
Why are you single; why live alone?
Have you no babies; have you no home?"

After the ball is over, after the break of morn,
After the dancers' leaving, after the stars are gone,
Many a heart is aching, if you could read them all,
Many the hopes that have vanished, after the ball.

"I have a story, sweetheart," he said,
"A story that's sad, and old as the days;
It was many years ago I loved a maid,
Beautiful, one who is now passed away."

title: The Sidewalks of New York
origin: Words by James W. Blake, music by Charles B. Lawlor, published 1894
note: A nostalgic waltz that became an unofficial anthem of New York City, later used as Al Smith's 1928 presidential campaign song. Public domain since 1894.
verses:
Down in front of Casey's old brown wooden stoop,
On a summer's evening we formed a merry group,
Boys and girls together, we would sing and waltz,
While Tony played the organ on the sidewalks of New York.

East side, west side, all around the town,
The tots sang 'ring-a-rosie', 'London Bridge is falling down,'
Boys and girls together, me and Mamie O'Rourke,
Tripped the light fantastic on the sidewalks of New York.

Things have changed since those times, some are up in 'G',
Others they are wand'rers, but they all feel just like me,
They'd part with all they've got, could they once more walk,
With their best girl and have a twirl on the sidewalks of New York.

title: In the Good Old Summer Time
origin: Words by Ren Shields, music by George Evans, published 1902
note: A hit of the early Tin Pan Alley era, still played at ballparks and county fairs. Public domain since 1902.
verses:
There's a time in each year that we always hold dear,
Good old summer time,
With the birds and the trees'es and sweet scented breezes,
Good old summer time.

In the good old summer time,
In the good old summer time,
Strolling through the shady lanes with your baby mine,
You hold her hand and she holds yours,
And that's a very good sign
That she's your tootsie wootsie in the good old summer time.

When your day's work is over then you are in clover,
And life is one beautiful rhyme,
No trouble annoying, each one is enjoying
The good old summer time.

title: Bill Bailey, Won't You Please Come Home
origin: Hughie Cannon, published 1902
note: One of the most recorded songs of the Tin Pan Alley era, later a jazz standard covered by Louis Armstrong, Bobby Darin, and countless others. Public domain since 1902.
verses:
On one summer's day, sun was shining fine,
The lady love of old Bill Bailey was hanging out clothes on the line,
In her back yard, and weeping hard,
She married a butcher, but he's her man,
And to a friend she said, in tears,"I'm all alone,"
I've been savin' this ad through the years.

Won't you come home, Bill Bailey, won't you come home?
She moans the whole day long,
I'll do the cooking, honey, I'll pay the rent,
I know I've done you wrong,
'Member that rainy evenin' I threw you out,
With nothin' but a fine tooth comb,
I know I'm to blame, well, ain't that a shame,
Bill Bailey, won't you please come home.

title: Meet Me in St. Louis, Louis
origin: Words by Andrew B. Sterling, music by Kerry Mills, published 1904
note: Written for the 1904 St. Louis World's Fair, and still associated with the city today. Public domain since 1904.
verses:
When Louis came home to the flat,
He hung up his coat and his hat,
He gazed all around, but no wifey he found,
So he said, "Where can Flossie be at?"
A note on the table he spied,
He read it just once, then he cried:
It read, "Louis dear, it's too slow for me here,
So I think I will go on my way."

Meet me in St. Louis, Louis,
Meet me at the fair,
Don't tell me the lights are shining
Any place but there;
We will dance the hoochee koochee,
I will be your tootsie wootsie,
If you will meet me in St. Louis, Louis,
Meet me at the fair.

"Meet me down at the Union Station,
Honey, dear, don't be late;
Meet me down at the Union Station,
By the big clock wait;
Meet me at the fountain there, dear,
Or I will meet you at the gate,
Meet me in St. Louis, Louis,
Meet me at the fair."

title: Red Wing
origin: Words by Thurland Chattaway, music by Kerry Mills, published 1907
note: One of the best-known 'Indian intermezzo' songs of the Tin Pan Alley era; its melody was later reused for the labor anthem 'Union Maid.' Public domain since 1907.
verses:
Now the moon shines tonight on pretty Red Wing,
The breeze is sighing, the night bird's crying,
Far away her warrior's sleeping,
While Red Wing's weeping her heart away.

She loved a warrior bold,
This shy little maid of old,
But brave and gay he rode one day,
To the battle far away.

Now the moon shines tonight on pretty Red Wing,
The breeze is sighing, the night bird's crying,
Far away her warrior's sleeping,
While Red Wing's weeping her heart away.

title: Take Me Out to the Ball Game
origin: Words by Jack Norworth, music by Albert Von Tilzer, published 1908
note: Sung during the seventh-inning stretch at ballparks across America; neither writer had attended a professional game before writing it. Public domain since 1908.
verses:
Katie Casey was baseball mad,
Had the fever and had it bad,
Just to root for the home town crew,
Ev'ry sou Katie blew.
On a Saturday, her young beau
Called to see if she'd like to go
To see a show, but Miss Kate said, "No,
I'll tell you what you can do."

Take me out to the ball game,
Take me out with the crowd,
Buy me some peanuts and cracker jack,
I don't care if I never get back,
Let me root, root, root for the home team,
If they don't win, it's a shame,
For it's one, two, three strikes, you're out,
At the old ball game.

title: Shine On, Harvest Moon
origin: Words by Jack Norworth, music by Nora Bayes, published 1908
note: Introduced by husband-and-wife vaudeville stars Norworth and Bayes; later a standard revived in film and jazz. Public domain since 1908.
verses:
I ain't had no lovin' since April, January, June, or July,
Snow time ain't no time to stay outdoors and spoon,
So shine on, shine on harvest moon, for me and my gal.

Shine on, shine on harvest moon,
Up in the sky,
I ain't had no lovin' since January, February, June or July,
Snow time ain't no time to stay outdoors and spoon,
So shine on, shine on harvest moon,
For me and my gal.

title: Home! Sweet Home!
origin: Words by John Howard Payne, music adapted by Henry Bishop, from the opera Clari, or the Maid of Milan, 1823
note: One of the most popular songs of the 19th century, played to remind soldiers of home on both sides of the Civil War (and reportedly banned from Union camps to prevent desertion). Public domain since 1823.
verses:
'Mid pleasures and palaces though we may roam,
Be it ever so humble, there's no place like home;
A charm from the skies seems to hallow us there,
Which, seek through the world, is ne'er met with elsewhere.

Home, home, sweet, sweet home!
There's no place like home!
There's no place like home!

title: The Last Rose of Summer
origin: Thomas Moore, published 1805 in his Irish Melodies, set to a traditional Irish air
note: One of Thomas Moore's most famous Irish Melodies, later quoted by Beethoven and Flotow. Public domain since its 1805 publication.
verses:
'Tis the last rose of summer,
Left blooming alone;
All her lovely companions
Are faded and gone;
No flower of her kindred,
No rosebud is nigh,
To reflect back her blushes,
Or give sigh for sigh.

I'll not leave thee, thou lone one,
To pine on the stem;
Since the lovely are sleeping,
Go, sleep thou with them;
Thus kindly I scatter
Thy leaves o'er the bed,
Where thy mates of the garden
Lie scentless and dead.

title: The Minstrel Boy
origin: Thomas Moore, published 1813 in his Irish Melodies, set to the traditional Irish air "The Moreen"
note: Written in memory of friends Moore lost in the 1798 Irish Rebellion; long a favorite at military funerals. Public domain since its 1813 publication.
verses:
The Minstrel Boy to the war is gone,
In the ranks of death you'll find him;
His father's sword he has girded on,
And his wild harp slung behind him.
"Land of song!" said the warrior bard,
"Though all the world betrays thee,
One sword, at least, thy rights shall guard,
One faithful harp shall praise thee!"

The Minstrel fell! But the foeman's chain
Could not bring his proud soul under;
The harp he loved ne'er spoke again,
For he tore its chords asunder;
And said, "No chains shall sully thee,
Thou soul of love and bravery!
Thy songs were made for the pure and free,
They shall never sound in slavery."

title: The Wild Rover
origin: Traditional British Isles song; earliest known printed text 1829 (as a temperance ballad), Roud Folk Song Index no. 1173
note: Often linked to the 19th-century temperance movement despite its rowdy reputation today; the traditional song has no identified author and is public domain.
verses:
I've been a wild rover for many a year,
And I've spent all my money on whiskey and beer,
But now I'm returning with gold in great store,
And I never will play the wild rover no more.

And it's no, nay, never,
No, nay, never, no more,
Will I play the wild rover,
No, never, no more.

I went into an alehouse I used to frequent,
And I told the landlady my money was spent,
I asked her for credit, she answered me "Nay,
Such a custom as yours I could have any day."

title: Paddy Works on the Railway
origin: Traditional Irish-American railroad work song; documented in print by 1864 as "Paddy on the Railway"
note: Sung by the Irish immigrant laborers who built much of the American and Canadian rail network in the mid-19th century. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
In eighteen hundred and forty-one,
I put my corduroy breeches on,
I put my corduroy breeches on,
To work upon the railway, the railway,
I'm weary of the railway,
Poor Paddy works on the railway.

In eighteen hundred and forty-two,
From Hartlepool I moved to Crewe,
And I found myself a job to do,
A-working on the railway.

In eighteen hundred and forty-three,
I broke my shin at the knee,
I broke my shin at the knee,
A-working on the railway.

title: Matty Groves
origin: Traditional English ballad, Child Ballad No. 81 ("Little Musgrave and Lady Barnard"), earliest printed broadside c. 1658-1664
note: One of the oldest English ballads in continuous circulation. Fairport Convention's landmark 1969 electric arrangement helped define British folk-rock.
verses:
A holiday, a holiday, and the first one of the year,
Lord Barnard's wife came into the church, the gospel for to hear.

And when the meeting it was done, the ladies all went home,
Then up and spoke a little foot page, by the fireside sitting alone.

'What news, what news, my little page, what news hast brought to me?'
'I bring you news, my mistress dear, sad news I bring to thee.'

'You must go home, Lord Barnard,' he cried, 'as fast as horse can run,
For Matty Groves is at your house, in bed with your wife alone.'

title: The House Carpenter
origin: Traditional Anglo-American ballad descended from "James Harris (The Daemon Lover)," Child Ballad No. 243; the earliest broadside is entered in the London Stationers' Register in 1657
note: A ghostly-lover ballad that crossed to America and shed most of its supernatural framing. Bob Dylan recorded it early in his career (1961-62), and it became a folk-revival and folk-rock standard.
verses:
'Well met, well met, my own true love,
Well met, well met,' cried he,
'I've just returned from the salt, salt sea,
And it's all for the love of thee.'

'I could have married a king's daughter there,
She would have married me,
But I refused the golden crown,
All for the love of thee.'

'If you could have married a king's daughter there,
I'm sure you're the one to blame,
For I am married to a house carpenter,
And I'm sure he's a fine young man.'

'Will you forsake your house carpenter,
And go along with me?
I'll take you where the grass grows green,
On the banks of the deep blue sea.'

title: The Golden Vanity
origin: Traditional English ballad, Child Ballad No. 286, thought to originate in a 17th-century broadside about Sir Walter Raleigh's ship the Sweet Trinity
note: A centuries-old maritime ballad of a cabin boy who sinks an enemy ship, then is betrayed by his own captain. Collected across England, Scotland, and Appalachia.
verses:
There was a little ship and she sailed upon the sea,
And the name of the ship was the Golden Vanity,
And she sailed upon the lowland, lonesome low,
And she sailed upon the lowland sea.

Then up spoke the cabin boy, and boldly outspoke he,
Said, 'What will you give me if I sink her in the sea?'
'I will give you gold, I will give you fee,
And my eldest daughter your bonny bride shall be.'

He turned upon his back and away swam he,
He swam till he came to the Golden Vanity,
But the captain would not take him up, so cruel was he,
And he left him in the lowland, lonesome low.

title: Pretty Polly
origin: Descended from the 18th-century English broadside ballad "The Gosport Tragedy," first printed c. 1760; the shortened Appalachian form documented in American folk collections by the early 20th century
note: One of the most-recorded murder ballads in bluegrass and old-time music. The text here follows the condensed traditional Appalachian form rather than any single copyrighted recording.
verses:
Pretty Polly, pretty Polly, come go along with me,
Pretty Polly, pretty Polly, come go along with me,
Before we get married some pleasure to see.

He led her over hills and valleys so deep,
He led her over hills and valleys so deep,
At length pretty Polly began to weep.

'Willie, oh Willie, I'm afraid of your ways,
Willie, oh Willie, I'm afraid of your ways,
The way that you've rambled, you'll lead me astray.'

He dug on her grave more than half of the night,
He dug on her grave more than half of the night,
And went home to his bed till the daylight.

title: Old Joe Clark
origin: Traditional American fiddle tune of Appalachian origin, thought to date from the late 19th century; documented in print by the 1910s-1920s
note: One of the most widely known American fiddle tunes, with hundreds of floating, often nonsensical verses swapped freely between performers. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Old Joe Clark, he had a house,
Fifteen stories high,
And every story in that house
Was filled with chicken pie.

Fare thee well, old Joe Clark,
Fare thee well, I'm gone,
Fare thee well, old Joe Clark,
Goodbye, Lucy, long.

I went down to old Joe's house,
He invited me to supper,
Stumped my toe on the table leg,
Stuck my nose in the butter.

title: The Cuckoo
origin: Traditional English folk song brought to Appalachia by early settlers; documented in England by the 18th century and collected in the American South by Cecil Sharp in the 1910s
note: A wandering-verse song about restlessness and unfaithful love, using the cuckoo as a symbol of a fickle lover. A staple of old-time claw-hammer banjo repertoire.
verses:
Oh, the cuckoo, she's a pretty bird,
She warbles as she flies,
She never hollers cuckoo,
Till the fourth day of July.

Jack o' diamonds, Jack o' diamonds,
I know you of old,
You've robbed my poor pockets,
Of silver and gold.

I've played cards in England,
I've played cards in Spain,
I'll bet you ten dollars
I'll beat you next game.

title: The Crawdad Song
origin: Traditional American folk song, documented in the South and Appalachia by the early 20th century; call-and-response "crawdad hole" verses collected by folklorists including John Lomax
note: A simple, widely varied call-and-response song sung across the American South, with countless regional and invented verses. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
You get a line and I'll get a pole, honey,
You get a line and I'll get a pole, babe,
You get a line and I'll get a pole,
We'll go down to the crawdad hole,
Honey, baby, mine.

Get up, old man, you slept too late, honey,
Get up, old man, you slept too late, babe,
Get up, old man, you slept too late,
Last piece of crawdad's on your plate,
Honey, baby, mine.

Sittin' on the ice till my feet got cold, honey,
Sittin' on the ice till my feet got cold, babe,
Sittin' on the ice till my feet got cold,
Watchin' that crawdad hole,
Honey, baby, mine.

title: Duncan and Brady
origin: Traditional murder ballad based on the killing of a St. Louis policeman on 6 October 1890; first commercially recorded 1929 by Wilmer Watts and His Lonely Eagles
note: Rooted in the same 1890s St. Louis underworld that produced "Stagolee" and "Frankie and Johnny." Carried into the folk revival by Lead Belly, Dave Van Ronk, and Bob Dylan.
verses:
Duncan Jarvis was tendin' the bar,
In walked Brady with a shinin' star,
Brady said to Duncan, 'You are under arrest,'
Duncan shot a hole in Brady's breast.

Brady fell down on the barroom floor,
'Now, please, Mr. Duncan, don't you shoot me no more,'
Women all cryin', ain't it a shame,
Shot King Brady, gonna shoot him again.

'Brady, Brady, Brady, you know you done wrong,
You broke in my grocery when the game was goin' on,
Knockin' down the window and tearin' down the door,
And now you're lyin' dead on the grocery floor.'

title: Little Maggie
origin: Traditional Appalachian folk song, likely originating in the late 1800s; earliest commercial recording 1928 by Grayson and Whitter
note: Part of the "Darling Corey" family of Appalachian "white blues" songs. A bluegrass jam-session staple, later reworked by Robert Plant on his 2010 album Band of Joy.
verses:
Oh, yonder stands little Maggie,
With a dram glass in her hand,
She's drinking away her troubles,
And a-courting some other man.

Oh, how can I ever stand it,
Just to see them two blue eyes,
A-shining in the moonlight,
Like two diamonds in the sky.

Last time I saw little Maggie,
She was sitting on the banks of the sea,
With a forty-four around her,
And a banjo on her knee.

Lay down your last gold dollar,
Lay down your gold watch and chain,
Little Maggie's gonna dance for daddy,
Listen to that old banjo ring.

title: Kumbaya
origin: Traditional African American Gullah spiritual from the Sea Islands of South Carolina and Georgia; earliest documented recordings made in 1926 by folklorist Robert Winslow Gordon
note: Its authorship was falsely claimed for decades by an evangelist who said he wrote it in the 1930s; the 1926 Library of Congress recordings, made years before that claim, confirm it as an older traditional Gullah spiritual.
verses:
Kumbaya, my Lord, kumbaya,
Kumbaya, my Lord, kumbaya,
Kumbaya, my Lord, kumbaya,
Oh Lord, kumbaya.

Someone's singing, Lord, kumbaya,
Someone's singing, Lord, kumbaya,
Someone's singing, Lord, kumbaya,
Oh Lord, kumbaya.

Someone's crying, Lord, kumbaya,
Someone's crying, Lord, kumbaya,
Someone's crying, Lord, kumbaya,
Oh Lord, kumbaya.

Someone's praying, Lord, kumbaya,
Someone's praying, Lord, kumbaya,
Someone's praying, Lord, kumbaya,
Oh Lord, kumbaya.

title: Little Sadie
origin: Traditional American murder ballad; earliest documented text collected 1922 in Joplin, Missouri by folklorist Vance Randolph, first commercially recorded 1928
note: Also known as "Bad Lee Brown." This is the older, traditional root of the song — distinct from "Cocaine Blues," a separately copyrighted 1947 reworking by Troy "Red" Arnall that Johnny Cash later made famous at Folsom Prison.
verses:
I went out last night, made a little round,
I met little Sadie, and I blowed her down,
Went back home and got into bed,
A forty-four smokeless under my head.

I began to think what a deed I'd done,
I grabbed my hat and away I run,
Made a good run but a little too slow,
They overtook me in Jericho.

I was standing there just a-taking my dram,
When up stepped the sheriff from Thomasville town,
He walked right up and he took me by the arm,
Said, 'Young man, you're under arrest.'

title: Jesse James
origin: Traditional American outlaw ballad; earliest known printed text published September 15, 1886 in the Daily Nebraska Press, four years after James's death
note: Written soon after the Missouri outlaw was shot by gang member Robert Ford in 1882, popularly (though not certainly) attributed to a songster named Billy Gashade. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Jesse James was a lad that killed many a man,
He robbed the Glendale train,
He stole from the rich and he gave to the poor,
He'd a hand and a heart and a brain.

Oh, Jesse had a wife to mourn all her life,
The children they were brave,
But that dirty little coward that shot Mr. Howard
Has laid poor Jesse in his grave.

It was his brother Frank that robbed the Gallatin bank,
And carried the money from the town,
It was in this very place that they had a little race,
For they shot Captain Sheets to the ground.

It was little Robert Ford, that dirty little coward,
I wonder how he does feel,
For he ate of Jesse's bread and he slept in Jesse's bed,
Then he laid poor Jesse in his grave.

title: Cole Younger
origin: Traditional American outlaw ballad; earliest documented text 1910, published in John Lomax's Cowboy Songs and Other Frontier Ballads
note: Sung from the perspective of Missouri outlaw Cole Younger, captured after the failed 1876 Northfield, Minnesota bank raid alongside the James brothers. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
I am a noble-minded man, Cole Younger is my name,
Of my many a depredation my friends I brought to shame,
For robbing of the Northfield bank the same I can't deny,
For it leaves me a poor prisoner, in the Stillwater jail to lie.

We started out for old Fort Scott, all in the state of Kansas,
For the purpose of avenging Quantrill and the rebel man's advance,
And we'd fight them anti-guerrillas until our dying day,
For the debt they owed our father's blood they never could repay.

It was in a Northfield, Minnesota, one Thursday afternoon,
Our pistols were a-drawing, and it filled us all with gloom,
For Charlie Pitts he was a hero, by our side he did remain,
While the citizens and the marshal, their bullets fell like rain.

title: Shady Grove
origin: Traditional Appalachian courting song, shares its melody with the older ballad "Matty Groves"; documented in southern mountain collections by the early 20th century
note: One of the most common old-time and bluegrass jam standards, with countless improvised verses swapped between players. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Shady Grove, my little love,
Shady Grove, I say,
Shady Grove, my little love,
Going away someday.

Cheeks as red as a blooming rose,
Eyes of the deepest brown,
You're the darling of my heart,
Stay 'til the sun goes down.

When I was a little boy,
I wanted a Barlow knife,
Now I want little Shady Grove,
To say she'll be my wife.

title: Cindy
origin: Traditional Appalachian courting/dance song of uncertain origin, likely 19th century; collected widely across the American South by the early 20th century
note: A widely varied square-dance favorite with countless floating verses, sung across the southern Appalachians for generations. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
You ought to see my Cindy,
She lives away down south,
She's so sweet the honey bees
Swarm around her mouth.

Get along home, Cindy, Cindy,
Get along home, Cindy, Cindy,
Get along home, Cindy, Cindy,
I'll marry you sometime.

She took me to the parlor,
She cooled me with her fan,
She swore I was the purtiest thing
In the shape of mortal man.

title: Darling Corey
origin: Traditional Appalachian folk song of the "white blues" family; earliest commercial recording 1918 (B.F. Shelton, 1927, following earlier field collections)
note: A moonshine-and-heartbreak ballad closely related to "Little Maggie," a bluegrass jam-session staple recorded by Bill Monroe, the Stanley Brothers, and Pete Seeger. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Wake up, wake up, darling Corey,
What makes you sleep so sound?
The revenue officers are coming,
Gonna tear your still house down.

Go away, go away, darling Corey,
Quit hanging around my bed,
Pretty women run me distracted,
Whiskey's gonna kill me dead.

The first time I saw darling Corey,
She was sitting on the banks of the sea,
Had a forty-four strapped around her body,
And a banjo on her knee.

title: Cripple Creek
origin: Traditional American fiddle tune, documented across the Appalachian and Ozark regions by the early 20th century
note: One of the most widely played beginner fiddle and banjo tunes in old-time music, with dozens of interchangeable verses. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
I've got a girl, lives up on the hill,
Won't work and her daddy won't,
Going up Cripple Creek, going in a run,
Going up Cripple Creek to have some fun.

Girls on Cripple Creek about half grown,
Jump on a boy like a dog on a bone,
Roll my britches to my knees,
Wade old Cripple Creek when I please.

title: Boil Them Cabbage Down
origin: Traditional Appalachian song with roots in African American work-song tradition; widely documented in southern folk collections by the early 20th century
note: A simple, endlessly extendable song used to teach beginning banjo and fiddle players, with a chorus more famous than any of its many interchangeable verses. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Boil them cabbage down, down,
Turn them hoecakes round, round,
The only song that I can sing
Is boil them cabbage down.

Possum up a 'simmon tree,
Rabbit on the ground,
Rabbit says, 'You son of a gun,
Shake them 'simmons down.'

title: The Arkansas Traveler
origin: Traditional American fiddle tune and comic dialogue, popularized in the 1840s-50s and widely printed by the 1860s; often attributed to Arkansas politician Sanford C. Faulkner
note: Originally a spoken comic routine between a traveler and a backwoods squatter, set to a well-known fiddle tune. One of the most recognizable American folk melodies. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Once upon a time in Arkansas,
An old man sat by his cabin door,
And fiddled at a tune without a name,
While his corn-patch was gone to weeds and shame.

'Say, old man, can you tell me where this road goes to?'
'It's never gone anywhere since I've lived here,
It's always right here where it is every mornin'.'
So the traveler learned to play the other half of that tune.

title: Green Grow the Rushes, O
origin: Traditional English cumulative counting song, structurally related to Hebrew "Echad Mi Yodea"; documented in England since at least the 18th century
note: A cumulative call-and-response song layering religious and folk symbolism verse by verse. Ancient and public domain.
verses:
I'll sing you one, O!
Green grow the rushes, O!
What is your one, O?
One is one and all alone and evermore shall be so.

I'll sing you two, O!
Green grow the rushes, O!
What are your two, O?
Two, two, the lily-white boys, clothed all in green, O,
One is one and all alone and evermore shall be so.

title: The Cherry Tree Carol
origin: Traditional English carol/ballad, Child Ballad No. 54, derived from the apocryphal Gospel of Pseudo-Matthew; documented in England from at least the 15th century
note: One of the oldest English Christmas carols, retelling an apocryphal story of Joseph and a pregnant Mary. Carried to Appalachia and preserved in the Southern mountain singing tradition.
verses:
Joseph was an old man, and an old man was he,
When he wedded Mary, in the land of Galilee.

Joseph and Mary walked through an orchard good,
Where was cherries and berries, so red as any blood.

'O then bespoke Mary, so meek and so mild,
"Pluck me one cherry, Joseph, for I am with child."

'O then bespoke Joseph, with words so unkind,
"Let him pluck thee a cherry that brought thee with child."'

title: The Twa Sisters (Binnorie)
origin: Traditional Scottish/English ballad, Child Ballad No. 10, with broadside versions dating to at least 1656
note: One of the darkest and most widespread ballads in the English tradition, found in hundreds of variants across the British Isles and North America. Ancient and public domain.
verses:
There were two sisters lived in a bower,
Binnorie, O Binnorie,
There came a knight to be their wooer,
By the bonnie mill-dams of Binnorie.

He courted the eldest with glove and ring,
Binnorie, O Binnorie,
But he loved the youngest above all thing,
By the bonnie mill-dams of Binnorie.

'O sister, sister, walk with me,
Binnorie, O Binnorie,
To see our father's ships come in,
By the bonnie mill-dams of Binnorie.'

She's ta'en her by the lily hand,
Binnorie, O Binnorie,
And thrown her into the river strand,
By the bonnie mill-dams of Binnorie.

title: Lord Randall
origin: Traditional Scottish/English ballad, Child Ballad No. 12, thought to date from at least the 17th century; collected across Britain, Ireland, Italy, and North America
note: A question-and-answer ballad in which a mother learns her son has been poisoned by his sweetheart. One of the most widespread ballads in Europe, and the model for Bob Dylan's "A Hard Rain's A-Gonna Fall."
verses:
'O where hae ye been, Lord Randall, my son?
O where hae ye been, my handsome young man?'
'I hae been at the greenwood; mother, mak my bed soon,
For I'm wearied wi hunting, and fain wad lie down.'

'What got ye to your dinner, Lord Randall, my son?
What got ye to your dinner, my handsome young man?'
'I gat eels boiled in broo; mother, mak my bed soon,
For I'm wearied wi hunting, and fain wad lie down.'

'And what became of your bloodhounds, Lord Randall, my son?
And what became of your bloodhounds, my handsome young man?'
'O they swelled and they died; mother, mak my bed soon,
For I'm wearied wi hunting, and fain wad lie down.'

'O I fear ye are poisoned, Lord Randall, my son!
O I fear ye are poisoned, my handsome young man!'
'O yes, I am poisoned; mother, mak my bed soon,
For I'm sick at the heart, and I fain wad lie down.'

title: The Trees They Do Grow High
origin: Traditional English/Scottish ballad (Roud 31), documented in broadsides from the early 19th century, likely older
note: A ballad of an arranged child marriage, sung from the bride's perspective as she waits for her young husband to grow up. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
The trees they do grow high, and the leaves they do grow green,
Many is the time my true love I've seen,
Many an hour I have watched him all alone,
He's young, but he's daily growing.

'O father, dear father, you've done to me great wrong,
You have married me to a boy who is too young,
I'm twice twelve and he is but fourteen,
He's young, but he's daily growing.'

'O daughter, dear daughter, I've done to you no wrong,
I have married you to a great lord's son,
He will be a match for you when I am dead and gone,
He's young, but he's daily growing.'

title: Come All Ye Fair and Tender Ladies
origin: Traditional Appalachian folk song assembled from older English broadside verses; documented in southern mountain collections by the early 20th century
note: A warning song to young women about the falseness of men, built from "floating verses" shared with many other traditional love laments. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Come all ye fair and tender ladies,
Take warning how you court young men,
They're like a star on a summer's morning,
They first appear and then they're gone.

They'll tell to you some loving story,
They'll make you think they love you well,
And away they'll go and court some other,
And leave you there in grief to dwell.

I wish I was a little sparrow,
And I had wings and I could fly,
Right to my false love's window,
I'd light on it and there I'd die.

title: Groundhog
origin: Traditional Appalachian hunting song, documented widely across the southern mountains by the early 20th century
note: A comic Appalachian hunting song describing the pursuit, cooking, and eating of a groundhog, sung by generations of old-time banjo players. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Get your gun and whistle up your dog,
Get your gun and whistle up your dog,
Get your gun and whistle up your dog,
We're goin' to the hills to catch a groundhog, groundhog.

Here comes Sal with a great big smile,
Here comes Sal with a great big smile,
Here comes Sal with a great big smile,
Sayin' groundhog gravy all the while, groundhog.

Take him home and skin him in the pot,
Take him home and skin him in the pot,
Take him home and skin him in the pot,
Call all the neighbors, whether or not, groundhog.

title: Salty Dog Blues
origin: Traditional American string-band standard, first commercially recorded 1924 by Papa Charlie Jackson ("Salty Dog Blues") and again in 1926 by the Allen Brothers
note: A foundational bluegrass and old-time jam standard, with Papa Charlie Jackson's 1924 recording among the earliest commercial blues records by an African American solo artist. Public domain since the 1920s.
verses:
Standing on the corner with the low-down blues,
A great big hole in the bottom of my shoes,
Honey, let me be your salty dog.

Salty dog, salty dog,
Honey, let me be your salty dog.

Look here, sugar, tell me who's your man,
And what in the world are you tryin' to prove?
Honey, let me be your salty dog.

title: Reuben's Train
origin: Traditional Appalachian railroad folk song, closely related to "900 Miles"; documented in southern mountain collections by the early 20th century
note: A wandering-verse railroad song shared across the American South, often blended with "900 Miles" and other train laments. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Oh, the train, the train, the Reuben train,
Oh, the train, the train, the Reuben train,
Oh, the train, the train, the Reuben train,
Done gone by.

Well, I'll be gone, oh, when you hear that whistle blow,
Well, I'll be gone, oh, when you hear that whistle blow,
A hundred miles, a hundred miles, a hundred miles.

title: The Unfortunate Rake
origin: Traditional Irish/English broadside ballad, documented from the late 18th century
note: The root ballad behind an entire family of American songs, including "St. James Infirmary Blues" and "Streets of Laredo." A soldier, dying of a disease from a life of dissipation, requests a grand military funeral. Ancient and public domain.
verses:
As I was a-walking down by the Lock Hospital,
As I was a-walking one morning of late,
Who did I spy but my own dear comrade,
Wrapped up in flannel, so hard was his fate.

'Had she but told me when she disordered me,
Had she but told me of it in time,
I might have got salts and pills of white mercury,
But now I'm cut down in the height of my prime.'

'Get six young soldiers to carry my coffin,
Get six young girls to bear up my pall,
Give them each bunches of roses to carry,
So they don't smell me as they carry me along.'

title: The Grey Goose
origin: Traditional African American tale-song, documented in Texas prison communities in the early 20th century; popularized nationally through Lead Belly's 1930s-40s field recordings for the Library of Congress
note: A tall-tale call-and-response song about an indestructible goose, sung to set a steady work rhythm. The traditional song itself, distinct from any single performer's specific recorded arrangement, is public domain.
verses:
Last Monday morning,
Lord, Lord, Lord,
Last Monday morning,
Lord, Lord, Lord,
My daddy went a-hunting,
Lord, Lord, Lord.

And he carried along his zulu,
Lord, Lord, Lord,
And along come a grey goose,
Lord, Lord, Lord.

He throwed it up to his shoulder,
Lord, Lord, Lord,
And he sight down that gun barrel,
Lord, Lord, Lord,
And he pull that trigger,
Lord, Lord, Lord.

title: Omie Wise
origin: Traditional North Carolina murder ballad based on the 1807/1808 drowning of Naomi Wise near Asheboro; the oldest known printed text appeared in the Greensboro Patriot, 29 April 1874
note: Considered one of the oldest documented American murder ballads, tied to a real, court-recorded case. Recorded by artists from Doc Watson to Bob Dylan to Elvis Costello.
verses:
Oh, listen to my story, I'll tell you no lies,
How John Lewis did murder poor little Omie Wise.

He told her to meet him at Adams's Springs,
He promised her money and other fine things.

'Have you got any money, or have you got any clothes?'
'No, I have no money, I'll tell you no lies,
But money won't clear you, John Lewis,' she cries.

He kissed her and hugged her and turned her around,
Then pushed her in deep water, where he knew she would drown.

title: John Hardy
origin: Traditional West Virginia murder ballad based on the 1893 crime and 1894 execution of railroad worker John Hardy in McDowell County; earliest commercial recordings 1924-1925
note: Often confused with the separate, older ballad "John Henry" due to the similar name and railroad setting, but based on a distinct, documented 1893 case. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
John Hardy was a desperate little man,
He carried two guns every day,
He shot down a man on the West Virginia line,
You oughta seen John Hardy getting away.

John Hardy stood in that old barroom,
So drunk that he could not see,
A man walked up and took him by the arm,
Said, 'Johnny, come and go along with me.'

John Hardy stood in his old jail cell,
The tears running down from his eyes,
He said, 'I've been the death of many a poor boy,
But my six-shooters never told a lie.'

title: Banks of the Ohio
origin: Traditional 19th-century American murder ballad, not tied to a specific documented case; earliest commercial recordings 1927-1928
note: A close cousin of "Omie Wise" and "Pretty Polly," telling of a jilted suitor who drowns his sweetheart. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
I asked my love to take a walk,
To walk a little way with me,
And as we walked, then we would talk,
All about our wedding day.

Only say that you'll be mine,
In no other's arms entwine,
Down beside where the waters flow,
Down by the banks of the Ohio.

I held a knife against her breast,
As into my arms she pressed,
She cried, 'My love, don't you murder me,
I'm not prepared for eternity.'

title: The Rocky Road to Dublin
origin: Written by D.K. Gavan, "The Galway Poet," for English music hall performer Harry Clifton, published in the 1860s
note: A rapid-fire comic travelogue of a journey from Tuam to Dublin to Liverpool. Long a session-tune and step-dance favorite. Public domain since its 1860s publication.
verses:
In the merry month of June, from my home I started,
Left the girls of Tuam, sure they were nearly broken-hearted,
Saluted father dear, kissed my darling mother,
Drank a pint of beer, my grief and tears to smother.

One, two, three, four, five,
Hunt the hare and turn her down the rocky road,
And all the way to Dublin, whack fol lol de rah!

In Mullingar that night I rested limbs so weary,
Started by daylight next morning, light and airy,
Took a drop of the pure, to keep my heart from sinking,
That's the Paddy's cure whenever he's thinking.

title: Finnegan's Wake
origin: Traditional Irish-American comic ballad, published as a broadside/sheet ballad in the mid-19th century
note: A comic ballad about a hod-carrier who falls to his apparent death, is waked by mourners, and revives when whiskey splashes on him during a fight. Later inspired the title of James Joyce's novel.
verses:
Tim Finnegan lived in Walkin Street,
A gentleman Irish mighty odd,
He had a brogue both rich and sweet,
And to rise in the world he carried a hod.

Whack fol the dah, dance to your partner,
Welt the floor, your trotters shake,
Isn't it the truth I told you,
Lots of fun at Finnegan's wake?

One morning Tim was rather full,
His head felt heavy which made him shake,
He fell from the ladder and he broke his skull,
So they carried him home, his corpse to wake.

title: The Parting Glass
origin: Traditional Scottish/Irish farewell song, dating to at least the 17th century; among the most popular songs in Scotland before "Auld Lang Syne" replaced it
note: Traditionally sung at the end of a gathering. An ancient farewell song with no known author, still commonly sung to close a night of music.
verses:
Of all the money e'er I had,
I spent it in good company,
And all the harm I've ever done,
Alas, it was to none but me.

So fill to me the parting glass,
Good night and joy be with you all.

Of all the comrades e'er I had,
They're sorry for my going away,
And all the sweethearts e'er I had,
They'd wish me one more day to stay.

title: I Know Where I'm Going
origin: Traditional Irish/Scottish folk song, published in versions from the early 20th century, drawing on older floating verses
note: A wistful love song widely collected across Ireland and Scotland in the early 1900s under various local titles. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
I know where I'm going,
And I know who's going with me,
I know who I love,
But the dear knows who I'll marry.

I have stockings of silk,
Shoes of fine green leather,
Combs to buckle my hair,
And a ring for every finger.

Feather beds are soft,
Painted rooms are bonny,
But I would give them all
For my handsome, winsome Johnny.

title: Sourwood Mountain
origin: Traditional Appalachian fiddle tune and dance song, documented across the southern mountains by the early 20th century
note: A staple square-dance and clogging tune with a distinctive nonsense refrain, endlessly extended with local, improvised verses. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Chickens a-crowing on Sourwood Mountain,
Hey ho, diddle-um-day,
So many pretty girls I can't count 'em,
Hey ho, diddle-um-day.

My true love lives up the holler,
Hey ho, diddle-um-day,
She won't come and I won't foller,
Hey ho, diddle-um-day.

title: Cluck Old Hen
origin: Traditional American fiddle and banjo tune, documented across the Appalachian and Ozark regions by the early 20th century
note: A beginner-friendly old-time standard, sung with comic, ever-changing verses about a cantankerous hen. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
My old hen's a good old hen,
She lays eggs for the railroad men,
Sometimes one, sometimes two,
Sometimes enough for the whole durn crew.

Cluck old hen, cluck and squall,
Ain't laid an egg since late last fall,
Cluck old hen, cluck old hen,
Didn't lay an egg for a poor old man.

title: Sally Goodin
origin: Traditional American fiddle tune, one of the oldest documented in Anglo-American fiddling; collected across the South and Midwest by the early 20th century
note: One of the most iconic American fiddle tunes, still a standard test piece at fiddle contests for its many creative variations. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Had a piece of pie and I had a piece of puddin',
And I gave it all away just to see Sally Goodin.

Some folks say a preacher won't steal,
I caught two in my cornfield,
One had a bushel, one had a peck,
One had a roastin' ear tied around his neck.

title: Ida Red
origin: Traditional American string-band song, first commercially recorded 1924 by Fiddlin' Powers and Family
note: A widely played square-dance standard that later fed directly into early rock and roll — Chuck Berry adapted its structure for "Maybellene" (1955). Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Ida Red, Ida Red,
I'm plumb fool about Ida Red,
Once I danced with Ida Red,
Now I'm dreamin' of Ida Red.

Ida Red, she's just my size,
Her eyes shine like the stars in the skies,
Ol' Ida Red, Ida Red,
Ol' Ida Red, Ida Red.

title: Little Liza Jane
origin: Traditional American folk song, likely originating in African American communities of the South; documented in print by the early 20th century, first commercially recorded in the 1910s-20s
note: A call-and-response favorite that crossed from parlor and minstrel-adjacent stages into the old-time and New Orleans jazz repertoires alike. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Come, my love, and live with me,
Little Liza Jane,
I will take good care of thee,
Little Liza Jane.

Oh, Eliza, Little Liza Jane,
Oh, Eliza, Little Liza Jane.

I've got a house in Baltimore,
Little Liza Jane,
Streetcar runs right by my door,
Little Liza Jane.

title: Frog Went A-Courtin'
origin: Traditional English/Scottish song, earliest known relative registered in the London Stationers' Register in 1548 as "A Moste Strange Weddinge of the Frogge and the Mowse"
note: One of the oldest documented folk songs in the English language, sung continuously for nearly 500 years in one form or another. Ancient and public domain.
verses:
Frog went a-courtin' and he did ride, uh-huh,
Frog went a-courtin' and he did ride,
Sword and pistol by his side, uh-huh.

He rode up to Miss Mousie's door, uh-huh,
He rode up to Miss Mousie's door,
Where he had been many times before, uh-huh.

'Miss Mouse, are you within?'
'Yes, kind sir, I sit and spin.'
'Please give to me your hand,'
Said the frog to the mouse, 'and I'll be your man.'

title: The Fox (Went Out on a Chilly Night)
origin: Traditional English folk song, documented in England since at least the 15th century; widely collected in American folk song collections by the early 20th century
note: An ancient comic ballad of a fox raiding a farmyard, sung continuously across England and America for centuries. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
The fox went out on a chilly night,
He prayed for the moon to give him light,
For he'd many a mile to go that night,
Before he reached the town-o, town-o, town-o,
He'd many a mile to go that night before he reached the town-o.

He ran till he came to a great big pen,
Where the ducks and the geese were kept therein,
'A couple of you will grease my chin,
Before I leave this town-o, town-o, town-o.'

He grabbed the grey goose by the neck,
He throwed a duck across his back,
He didn't mind their quack, quack, quack,
And their legs all danglin' down-o, down-o, down-o.

title: Billy Boy
origin: Traditional English/American folk song, descended from an older Scottish ballad, "Lord Randal"; widely collected in England and America by the 19th century
note: A comic question-and-answer courting song, structurally related to the much darker "Lord Randall." Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Oh, where have you been, Billy Boy, Billy Boy?
Oh, where have you been, charming Billy?
I have been to seek a wife, she's the joy of my life,
But she's a young thing and cannot leave her mother.

Did she ask you to come in, Billy Boy, Billy Boy?
Did she ask you to come in, charming Billy?
Yes, she asked me to come in, there's a dimple in her chin,
But she's a young thing and cannot leave her mother.

Can she bake a cherry pie, Billy Boy, Billy Boy?
Can she bake a cherry pie, charming Billy?
She can bake a cherry pie, quick as a cat can wink an eye,
But she's a young thing and cannot leave her mother.

title: The Riddle Song (I Gave My Love a Cherry)
origin: Traditional English folk song brought to Appalachia by early settlers; the riddle format traces to medieval English literature, documented in the mountain South by the early 20th century
note: A gentle riddle song answering its own puzzles in the final verse (a cherry with no stone is a cherry in bloom; a baby with no crying is a baby still sleeping). Ancient and public domain.
verses:
I gave my love a cherry that has no stone,
I gave my love a chicken that has no bone,
I gave my love a ring that has no end,
I gave my love a baby with no cryin'.

How can there be a cherry that has no stone?
How can there be a chicken that has no bone?
How can there be a ring that has no end?
How can there be a baby with no cryin'?

A cherry when it's bloomin', it has no stone,
A chicken when it's pippin', it has no bone,
A ring when it's rollin', it has no end,
A baby when it's sleepin', has no cryin'.

title: In the Sweet By and By
origin: Words by S. Fillmore Bennett, music by Joseph P. Webster, published 1868
note: Legend holds that Webster composed the tune within thirty minutes of Bennett writing the words, at a Wisconsin drugstore. One of the most widely sung American gospel hymns. Public domain since 1868.
verses:
There's a land that is fairer than day,
And by faith we can see it afar,
For the Father waits over the way,
To prepare us a dwelling place there.

In the sweet by and by,
We shall meet on that beautiful shore,
In the sweet by and by,
We shall meet on that beautiful shore.

We shall sing on that beautiful shore
The melodious songs of the blest,
And our spirits shall sorrow no more,
Not a sigh for the blessing of rest.

title: What Wondrous Love Is This
origin: Traditional American shape-note hymn, text traced to a 1811 camp-meeting songbook; first appeared with its familiar melody in William Walker's Southern Harmony, 1835
note: One of the great American folk hymns, sung in the haunting Dorian mode of Sacred Harp shape-note singing. Anonymous and public domain.
verses:
What wondrous love is this, O my soul, O my soul,
What wondrous love is this, O my soul,
What wondrous love is this, that caused the Lord of bliss
To bear the dreadful curse for my soul, for my soul,
To bear the dreadful curse for my soul.

To God and to the Lamb, I will sing, I will sing,
To God and to the Lamb, I will sing,
To God and to the Lamb, who is the great I Am,
While millions join the theme, I will sing, I will sing,
While millions join the theme, I will sing.

And when from death I'm free, I'll sing on, I'll sing on,
And when from death I'm free, I'll sing on,
And when from death I'm free, I'll sing and joyful be,
And through eternity, I'll sing on, I'll sing on,
And through eternity, I'll sing on.

title: Another Man Done Gone
origin: Traditional African American prison work song from the Mississippi Delta; first documented and recorded by folklorists John and Alan Lomax in the 1930s-40s
note: A stark, minimal work song about escape and violence in the Southern prison system. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Another man done gone,
Another man done gone,
Another man done gone from the county farm,
Another man done gone.

I didn't know his name,
I didn't know his name,
I didn't know his name, I didn't know his name,
I didn't know his name.

He had a long chain on,
He had a long chain on,
He had a long chain on, he had a long chain on,
He had a long chain on.

title: Long John
origin: Traditional African American prison work song, documented by folklorists in Texas and Louisiana prison farms in the 1930s
note: A rhythmic axe- or hoe-work song about a man on the run from prison, sung to coordinate the pace of manual labor. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Long John, he's long gone,
Like a turkey through the corn,
With his long clothes on,
Like a turkey through the corn.

He's long gone, he's long gone,
He's long gone,
Big black rabbit ran a hundred mile,
He's long gone.

Well, some folks say he never was born,
Just growed up like a turnip greens,
He's long gone, he's long gone,
Like a turkey through the corn.

title: The Butcher Boy
origin: Traditional English/American ballad, assembled from older broadside verses circulating in England by the 18th century; documented widely in American folk collections by the early 20th century
note: A tragic ballad of a young woman abandoned by her lover, built from floating verses shared with several other traditional laments. Ancient and public domain.
verses:
In London city where I did dwell,
A butcher boy I loved so well,
He courted me my life away,
And now with me he will not stay.

There is a place in this same town,
Where my love goes and sits himself down,
He takes another girl on his knee,
And tells to her what he told to me.

She went upstairs to make her bed,
And not a word to her mother said,
Her mother she went upstairs to see,
And found her hanging from a tree.

title: Black Is the Color of My True Love's Hair
origin: Traditional Scottish-American folk song; lyrics collected by Cecil Sharp in Kentucky in 1917, likely older
note: A haunting Appalachian love song of Scottish descent. Presented here in the traditional lyric form documented by Cecil Sharp's 1917 field collection, predating any individually copyrighted 20th-century melodic arrangement.
verses:
Black is the color of my true love's hair,
Her lips are like some roses fair,
She has the sweetest smile and the gentlest hands,
And I love the ground whereon she stands.

I love my love and well she knows,
I love the ground whereon she goes,
I wish the day it soon would come,
When she and I could be as one.

I go to the Clyde and I mourn and weep,
For satisfied I ne'er can be,
I write her a letter, just a few short lines,
And suffer death a thousand times.

title: Haul Away Joe
origin: Traditional halyard shanty, documented aboard British and American sailing ships by the mid-19th century
note: A short-drag shanty for hauling square-rig halyards, sung with a hard pull on 'Joe.' No individual author identified. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Away, haul away, we'll haul away Joe,
Away, haul away, we'll haul away Joe,
Once I had a scolding wife, and she wouldn't do for me,
Away, haul away, we'll haul away Joe.

I traded her for a barrel of flour, and things went better then,
Away, haul away, we'll haul away Joe,
When I was a little lad, my mother always told me,
Away, haul away, we'll haul away Joe.

King Louis was the King of France before the Revolution,
Away, haul away, we'll haul away Joe,
But then he got his head cut off, which spoiled his constitution,
Away, haul away, we'll haul away Joe.

title: South Australia
origin: Traditional capstan shanty, likely originating among wool-trade sailors on the Australian run in the 1880s
note: Sung to heave the anchor windlass on ships bound to and from Australia. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
In South Australia I was born,
Heave away, haul away,
In South Australia, round Cape Horn,
We're bound for South Australia.

Heave away, haul away,
Heave away, haul away,
Heave away, you rolling king,
We're bound for South Australia.

As I walked out one morning fair,
Heave away, haul away,
It's there I met Miss Nancy Blair,
We're bound for South Australia.

title: Rio Grande
origin: Traditional capstan shanty, documented aboard sailing ships in the mid-to-late 19th century
note: A homeward-bound shanty sung while weighing anchor for the Rio Grande do Sul cattle trade. No author identified; traditional and public domain.
verses:
Oh, say, were you ever in Rio Grande?
Away, you Rio,
Oh, say, were you ever in Rio Grande?
We're bound for the Rio Grande.

And away, you Rio, away, you Rio,
Sing fare you well, my bonny young girl,
We're bound for the Rio Grande.

So it's pack up your donkey and haul up your gear,
Away, you Rio,
We're bound away from Liverpool here,
We're bound for the Rio Grande.

title: Boney Was a Warrior
origin: Traditional halyard shanty commemorating Napoleon Bonaparte, likely originating among early-19th-century sailors
note: A shanty tracing Napoleon's career from Corsica to his 1821 death on St. Helena, sung by sailors for generations after the fact. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Boney was a warrior,
Away, ay-ah,
A warrior, a terrier,
Jean Francois.

Boney fought the Prussians,
Away, ay-ah,
Boney fought the Russians,
Jean Francois.

Boney went to Moscow,
Away, ay-ah,
Moscow was a-blazing,
Jean Francois.

Boney went to Elba,
Away, ay-ah,
Boney he came back again,
Jean Francois.

title: Roll the Old Chariot Along
origin: Traditional stamp-and-go shanty, adapted from the African American spiritual "Roll the Old Chariot"; documented aboard sailing ships by the late 19th century
note: A rousing hauling shanty adapted from a camp-meeting spiritual, with endless improvised verses ending 'and we'll all hang on behind.' Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Oh, a drop of Nelson's blood wouldn't do us any harm,
And we'll all hang on behind!

Oh, a drop of Nelson's blood wouldn't do us any harm,
And we'll all hang on behind!
And we'll roll the old chariot along,
And we'll roll the old chariot along,
And we'll roll the old chariot along,
And we'll all hang on behind!

Oh, a nice fat cook wouldn't do us any harm,
And we'll all hang on behind!
And we'll roll the old chariot along.

title: A-Roving
origin: Traditional capstan shanty, its verses derived from a much older 17th-century English song, "The Maiden's Complaint"; documented in shanty collections by the late 19th century
note: One of the oldest shanty melodies still commonly sung, adapted from a Jacobean-era comic song. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
In Amsterdam there lived a maid,
Mark well what I do say,
In Amsterdam there lived a maid,
And she was mistress of her trade,
I'll go no more a-roving with you, fair maid.

A-roving, a-roving,
Since roving's been my ruin,
I'll go no more a-roving,
With you, fair maid.

Her eyes are like two stars so bright,
Mark well what I do say,
Her eyes are like two stars so bright,
Her face it is her fortune quite,
I'll go no more a-roving with you, fair maid.

title: Sir Patrick Spens
origin: Traditional Scottish ballad, Child Ballad No. 58, possibly referencing a 13th-century royal voyage; the earliest printed text appeared in 1765 in Bishop Thomas Percy's Reliques of Ancient English Poetry
note: Widely regarded as one of the finest of the Scottish popular ballads, telling of a doomed sea voyage ordered by a king. Ancient and public domain.
verses:
The king sits in Dunfermline town,
Drinking the blude-red wine;
'O whar will I get a guid sailor,
To sail this ship of mine?'

'To Noroway, to Noroway,
To Noroway o'er the faem;
The king's daughter of Noroway,
'Tis thou maun bring her hame.'

'O wha is this has done this deed,
This ill deed done to me,
To send me out this time o' the year,
To sail upon the sea?'

'Late late yestre'en I saw the new moone,
Wi' the auld moone in hir arme,
And I feir, I feir, my deir master,
That we will cum to harme.'

title: Geordie
origin: Traditional English/Scottish ballad, Child Ballad No. 209, with printed broadsides dating to the 17th century
note: A ballad of a woman pleading for the life of her condemned lover. Widely collected across the British Isles and Appalachia in dozens of variants. Ancient and public domain.
verses:
As I walked out over London Bridge,
One misty morning early,
I overheard a fair pretty maid,
Was lamenting for her Geordie.

'O my Geordie will be hanged in a golden chain,
'Tis not the chain of many,
He was born of King's royal breed,
And lost to a virtuous lady.'

'Go bridle me my milk-white steed,
Go bridle me my pony,
I will ride to London's court,
To plead for the life of Geordie.'

title: The Bonnie Earl of Moray
origin: Traditional Scottish ballad, Child Ballad No. 181, commemorating the 1592 murder of James Stewart, 2nd Earl of Moray
note: One of the few Child ballads tied to a precisely dated historical event — the earl's assassination in a political feud. Sung continuously in Scotland for over 400 years. Ancient and public domain.
verses:
Ye Highlands and ye Lowlands,
Oh, where hae ye been?
They hae slain the Earl of Moray,
And laid him on the green.

'Now wae be to thee, Huntly,
And wherefore did you sae?
I bade you bring him wi' you,
But forbade you him to slay.'

He was a braw gallant,
And he rid at the ring;
And the bonnie Earl of Moray,
Oh, he might have been a king.

title: The Zebra Dun
origin: Traditional American cowboy ballad, first published in John Lomax's Cowboy Songs and Other Frontier Ballads, 1910
note: A comic ballad of a scholarly stranger who astonishes cowhands by breaking a wild outlaw horse. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
We were camped on the plains at the head of the Cimarron,
When along came a stranger and stopped to arger some,
He looked so very foolish that we began to look around,
We thought he was a greenhorn that had just 'scaped from town.

We asked him if he had been raised way out in the West,
And he said he had left home for reasons of his own,
An old bald-face outlaw the boys called Zebra Dun,
We couldn't hire a man that could ride him for love or money.

He mounted that old outlaw and curled his hair back,
He emptied both his pockets, all but a chew of tobacco,
Old Dun found he couldn't run away and quit,
He went to bucking, then he began to weave and pitch.

title: Blood on the Saddle
origin: Traditional American cowboy song, likely descended from an older lumberjack ballad; documented in Western folklore collections by the early 20th century
note: A darkly comic cowboy song describing a fatal riding accident, sung with deadpan repetition. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
There was blood on the saddle,
And blood on the ground,
And a great big puddle,
Of blood all around.

A cowboy lay in it,
All covered with gore,
And he ne'er will ride,
Any broncos no more.

Oh, pity the cowboy,
All bloody and red,
For the bronco fell on him,
And bashed in his head.

title: Doney Gal
origin: Traditional American cowboy trail song, collected by John and Alan Lomax in their Western folk song collections of the early 20th century
note: A gentle night-herding song sung to soothe cattle and pass the long hours of a trail drive. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Oh, the moon is climbing high, love,
Stars are in the sky, love,
And it's pretty good to be out on the night herd,
Ridin' with my Doney Gal.

Oh, we'll drive them on to Kansas,
Where the girls all dance the Kansas,
And there's likker there that's fit for any man,
Ridin' with my Doney Gal.

When the day's work is all over,
And we've fed the boys their chuck,
We'll bed the cattle down and sing 'em soft and low,
Ridin' with my Doney Gal.

title: Ezekiel Saw the Wheel
origin: Traditional African American spiritual based on the vision in the Book of Ezekiel; documented in spiritual collections by the early 20th century
note: A joyful spiritual drawing on the prophet Ezekiel's vision of a wheel in the sky. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
Ezekiel saw the wheel,
Way up in the middle of the air,
Ezekiel saw the wheel,
Way in the middle of the air.

And the little wheel run by faith,
And the big wheel run by the grace of God,
A wheel in a wheel,
Way in the middle of the air.

Some go to church for to sing and shout,
Before six months they are all turned out,
Way in the middle of the air.

title: This Train
origin: Traditional African American gospel song, first documented in field recordings and collections of the 1920s-30s
note: A gospel train song using the railroad as a metaphor for the journey to salvation, listing who is and isn't welcome aboard. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
This train is bound for glory, this train,
This train is bound for glory, this train,
This train is bound for glory,
Don't ride nothin' but the righteous and the holy,
This train is bound for glory, this train.

This train don't carry no gamblers, this train,
This train don't carry no gamblers, this train,
This train don't carry no gamblers,
No hypocrites, no side-street ramblers,
This train don't carry no gamblers, this train.

This train don't carry no liars, this train,
This train don't carry no liars, this train,
This train don't carry no liars,
No hypocrites and no high flyers,
This train don't carry no liars, this train.

title: Just a Closer Walk with Thee
origin: Traditional African American gospel song, likely originating in 19th-century Southern churches; author unknown
note: Perhaps the most frequently played hymn in New Orleans jazz funerals. A 1940 arrangement by Kenneth Morris popularized the song nationally, but the traditional words and melody themselves are of unknown, older authorship and are in the public domain.
verses:
I am weak but Thou art strong,
Jesus, keep me from all wrong,
I'll be satisfied as long
As I walk, let me walk close to Thee.

Just a closer walk with Thee,
Grant it, Jesus, is my plea,
Daily walking close to Thee,
Let it be, dear Lord, let it be.

Through this world of toil and snares,
If I falter, Lord, who cares?
Who with me my burden shares?
None but Thee, dear Lord, none but Thee.

title: Rock My Soul in the Bosom of Abraham
origin: Traditional African American spiritual, documented in spiritual collections of the late 19th and early 20th centuries
note: A joyful, driving spiritual drawing on the parable of Lazarus and Abraham's bosom from the Gospel of Luke. Traditional and public domain.
verses:
So high, you can't get over it,
So low, you can't get under it,
So wide, you can't get round it,
Oh, rock my soul.

Rock my soul in the bosom of Abraham,
Rock my soul in the bosom of Abraham,
Rock my soul in the bosom of Abraham,
Oh, rock my soul.

title: Nelly Bly
origin: Stephen Foster, published 1849 by Firth, Pond & Co., New York
note: A cheerful, upbeat song written for Christy's Minstrels, distinct from and predating the famous journalist Nellie Bly (born 1864), who took her pen name from this earlier song. Public domain since 1849.
verses:
Nelly Bly shuts her eye when she goes to sleep,
When she wakens up again, her eyes begin to peep,
High, Nelly, ho, Nelly, listen love to me,
I'll sing for you, play for you, a dulcem melody.

Nelly Bly has a voice like a turtle dove,
I hear it in the meadow and I hear it in the grove,
Nelly Bly has a heart warm as cup of tea,
And bigger than the sweet potato down in Tennessee.

Heigh, Nelly, ho, Nelly, listen, love, to me,
I'll sing for you, play for you, a dulcem melody,
Heigh, Nelly, ho, Nelly, listen love to me,
I'll sing for you, play for you, a dulcem melody.

title: Come Where My Love Lies Dreaming
origin: Stephen Foster, published 1855 by Firth, Pond & Co., New York
note: A gentle vocal quartet piece, written for the popular parlor genre of harmonized part-singing. Public domain since 1855.
verses:
Come where my love lies dreaming,
Dreaming the happy hours away,
In visions bright redeeming,
The fleeting joys of day.

Come where my love lies dreaming,
Dreaming the happy hours away,
Come where my love lies dreaming,
Dreaming the happy hours away.

Weary hearts, dream on together,
Summer days will smile again,
Come where my love lies dreaming,
Dreaming the happy hours away.

title: Some Folks
origin: Stephen Foster, published 1855 by Firth, Pond & Co., New York
note: A lighthearted contrast between gloomy folks and the cheerful narrator, one of Foster's more purely comic numbers. Public domain since 1855.
verses:
Some folks like to sigh, some folks do,
Some folks are always in the blues,
Some folks love to sigh, but as for me,
I keep as merry as the day is long.

Some folks like to weep, some folks do,
Some folks lose their courage too,
But as for me I keep a laughing face,
And sing along all the day.

Then let the world go round and round,
And let the sun shine bright,
I'll do my best each day to smile,
And sing away till night.

title: Waiting for the Robert E. Lee
origin: Words by L. Wolfe Gilbert, music by Lewis F. Muir, published 1912
note: A ragtime-era hit celebrating a Mississippi steamboat race, later a jazz standard. Public domain since 1912.
verses:
Way down on the levee, in old Alabamy,
There's Daddy and Mammy, there's Ephraim and Sammy,
On a moonlight night you'll find them all,
They've got a date, so at the gate they stall.

Watch them shufflin' along,
Go, hear that mournful song,
They're stoppin' there,
They're proud of that old man,
That old man,
Run, get your grip, get on the ship,
For tomorrow day we're gonna have a big time.

Watch for the Robert E. Lee,
Soon you will see,
Comin' round the bend,
Signal 'er please, through the trees,
You'll see her smoke a'bendin'.

title: By the Beautiful Sea
origin: Words by Harold Atteridge, music by Harry Carroll, published 1914
note: A carefree seaside singalong that became a fixture of boardwalk sheet-music culture. Public domain since 1914.
verses:
By the sea, by the sea, by the beautiful sea,
You and I, you and I, oh how happy we'll be,
When each wave comes a-rolling in,
We will duck or swim,
And we'll float and fool around the water.

Over and under, and then up for air,
Pa is rich, ma is rich, so now what do we care,
I love to be beside your side,
Beside the seaside,
By the beautiful sea.

title: Ain't We Got Fun
origin: Words by Gus Kahn and Raymond B. Egan, music by Richard A. Whiting, published 1921
note: A cheerfully ironic Jazz Age song about being broke but happy, later quoted in F. Scott Fitzgerald's The Great Gatsby. Public domain since 1921.
verses:
Every morning, every evening,
Ain't we got fun?
Not much money, oh, but honey,
Ain't we got fun?

The rent's unpaid, dear, we haven't a car,
But anyhow we've got fun,
There's nothing surer, the rich get rich and the poor get children,
In the meantime, in between time,
Ain't we got fun?

Landlord, landlord, my roof needs a-mendin',
Before the place I live falls down,
I can pay the rent, but the landlord's a gent,
And he says he's satisfied, if I ain't got fun.

title: Toot, Toot, Tootsie (Goo' Bye)
origin: Words and music by Gus Kahn, Ernie Erdman, and Dan Russo, published 1922
note: A Jazz Age farewell song, famously performed by Al Jolson in The Jazz Singer (1927), the first feature-length film with synchronized dialogue. Public domain since 1922.
verses:
Toot, toot, Tootsie, goo'bye,
Toot, toot, Tootsie, don't cry,
The choo choo train that takes me,
Away from you, no words can tell how sad it makes me.

Kiss me, Tootsie, and then,
Do it over again,
Watch for the mail,
I'll never fail,
If you don't get a letter then you know I'm in jail.

Think of me, Tootsie, when the lights are low,
Dream of the day that I'll be with you again,
Watch for the mail, I'll never fail,
If you don't get a letter then you know I'm in jail.
